There's non-buyer leads and buyer leads. OK, non-buyer leads are going to be opt-ins for VSLs, webinars, maybe they're putting in a number for your Facebook group, your mini chat, whatever. It's opt-ins for a free resource. Now, because they're not actually whooping out their credit card, putting in their credit card digits, their address, all of that stuff, speed is the key for non-buyers. OK, we're going to get more into this later. These leads can be effective and they can turn into sales. It's just that you need to contact them a lot more quickly. And you have far more leniency with buyer leads. OK, and buyer leads are going to be low ticket products, $7 to $297, or mid-ticket even, $1,000 to $2,000. We've had guys create an outbound team around taking an $1,000 to $2,000 course buyer and turning that into a high ticket sale. So you can certainly do that. Or the model that I see most often with low ticket products is like $50 product to consult to like 10k sale, for instance. So that would be a buyer lead. Now, with these, you have far more leniency in terms of speed. Because if you just think about it, with an opt-in, if it even goes five, six, seven days, they're probably going to figure out who you are. Because it's just a free guy. They're just nosing around. They probably didn't even look in their email or look through it or whatever. Or it's a free training with something that they actually whooped out their credit card and purchased. It's a totally different story. Because whether that was a book that they're expecting, whether it was a $50 digital product, a $297 product, they probably remember buying it. And they've either been through it or they're planning on going through it. So they're going to remember at least who you are when you call. So because of that, you have far more leniency with speed. Now, I don't see any reason why you wouldn't still want to dial within 10, 15 minutes, which we're going to talk about in a second. The other thing with buyer leads is you have higher compliance because of the customer service frame. So for instance, when you're calling for an opt-in, if they download a free guide and you're calling them, they probably know pretty quickly that you're a sales person. When your calling is for a buyer lead, somebody who's actually bought in a lower mid-ticket product, they don't know if you're calling about their shipment or your order or access to the product or a bonus they didn't get. ## [03:01 - 06:02]

in with a buyer lead, which makes sense. work better for you. So basically what we do, once a lead comes in, it's instantly zapped into a Slack channel. Now you can see here. Now they comment on this once they've made that first dial, and they can make two or three more calls within the first two hours. After the first two hours, the lead's going to get assigned to a specific salesperson in the CRM. But the issue with that is that it doesn't really, like I want the lead when it comes in to be in their face, so they're able to dial that immediately. But after two hours, we just do the typical thing, where leads assigned to a specific salesperson in the CRM, it's no longer free for all. That's their lead. And then from there, the salesperson is going to follow up with their lead list via the power dialer for the trailing seven days of opt-ins. So if it's just an opt-in for a free guide, we're only going to dial back seven days. If it's a buyer, we'll dial back 15. So if you do have opt-ins that you want to call and buyers, you're going to want different segments for both, so they can choose the quality of the lead that they're dialing. ## [09:03 - 12:05]

about optimizing your time, et cetera. And if you're watching this as a business owner, training your team shouldn't be about you have to make this many dials a day. Or you can set KPIs on that eventually, depending on your company. But it shouldn't be like you have to dial back seven days every single day. If they have, for instance, two steps, the Free For All channel, their group joins, and other leads that are very, very high quality, like I want them to focus their time on what's going to make them the most money, because that's going to make me the most money. So I want them to be more focused on achieving the outcome and putting points on the board than the process. So hopefully, this makes sense, because when you put too much process, especially on your outbound team, to where you make them do a certain amount of dials and a certain amount of leads every single day, and they have kind of no freedom to go free reign and find the leads that are best for them and focus their time on what's going to produce the most results for them, what happens is the results go down. OK? So if you have questions about that, let me know. But that is very, very key. I was once on a team where the sales manager, he implemented a system where a certain amount of leads had to be called in a certain amount of order every single day without fail. And because they prioritized that process over just what was putting points on the board, all the results of the entire team tanked. OK? So if you're running a dual Facebook group and a call model to where you have leads coming in that are being outbound out, but at the same time, you're doing things like two steps and you're reaching out to group joins, et cetera, you want them to be incentivized to just do whatever is going to put the points on the board. OK? Because most times, these group joins, they're the most valuable leads by far. Like, they'd rather spend the time doing the group joins and getting that person on the calendar. It's way more efficient than doing an outbound dial. Right? Same thing with a two step. And same thing if it's a free for all hot lead that just opted in, you want them calling that person, not calling somebody you opted in for a free guide seven days ago. So it's prioritizing what are the hottest opportunities and working their way down. So I know I beat that one to death there, but hopefully that makes sense. So once they call in the free for all, and they drop a comment and let them know they called, after that it's going to get assigned. So that's one option. And we're doing that right now successfully. buffer period is that any rep who's available can make the dial. So for instance, so-and-so might be on back-to-back triage calls for two hours, and they totally missed the lead that came in. But I do know that these are pretty solid numbers, and you should be able to hit these, especially with a buyer's list. With an opt-in list, I think you can still hit these, but you're going to have to focus more on speed. Or you might be able to hit these. You might be able to get close to these, but your result isn't going to be as much as it probably is with buyers. So dials, obviously I just said don't set a super big process on them. But most all outbound salespeople should be able to do at least 100 hours a day, or 100 hours, 100 calls per day. OK, so that should be the minimum in most cases. Obviously, if there's some crazy heavy opportunity in the Facebook group, I wouldn't come down on the salesperson if they're spending a lot of time on there, and they're producing. But if they're not producing sets, and they're not hitting $100 per day, then you want to come down on them for sure. So on this note, one of the things I always taught the outbound teams I trained is really the power hour method to where, if they're balancing out, let's say, mini chat versus their power dialer versus the Facebook group, et cetera, what you want them to do is basically set an hour where they only do messenger-based opportunity, set that timer for 60 minutes, then an hour where they only do power dialing, you set that timer for 60 minutes, and then they switch with 10 minute breaks in between. So when you set that timer, just set a timer on your phone, 60 minutes, let it go to zero, and then you only focus on that one activity, either calling or messenger each time, that allows you to get, I mean, usually, two, three power hours most. You can hit this 100 dials, and then you can spend the rest of the three or four hours. Facebook, Messenger, mini chat should be plenty. And the guys I had doing this system were producing five, six, seven sets a day. So I know it works. Now, $100 per day out of 100, 20% answer rates, that's going to be 20 live people answering the phone,

## [15:07 - 18:09]

one out of every five. Qualified answers is 5%. So basically, out of the 20 answers, five are going to be qualified. We define qualified as they have a problem you can solve, they have money, and they're open to help. Now, when I say they have money, we're not like hard qualifying here on their credit score and how much money they have in their bank account on an outbound dial. But look, if the person's working at Walmart, they probably don't have money. So this is just like, we just want to make sure there's no glaringly red flags here. Sets in terms of how many of these five the answer should turn into a set, we want to go four out of every five. So that would be 4% of the total volume. Now, really, once the salesperson is fully ramped, if they're good, if they talk to a qualified person, they should set them 80% to 90% of the time. So you should have maybe one or two out of every five qualified answers tops that just aren't open to help. Because if they're qualified, they have a problem you can solve, they have money, and they're open to help. The only reason they're not going to get set, if you're good, is they're just ego, not open to help, not in the market. Now, closes 30% to 40%, which is going to be four sets out of four sets, one to two should close. So the close right here is not 30% to 40% on the 100 calls, it's 30% to 40% on the sets that are made. So this is closing ratio. So once the setter is good, really what we found is that the closing ratio of the setter that's good should be really actually better than what you experience from inbound leads specifically. So I've seen setters who I've trained have their sets closed consistently within the 40% to 60% range.
